papers.title: JuliusBrussee/caveman — Claude Code skill that cuts 65% of output tokens by talking like caveman
papers.notes: Prompt-based output compression (not algorithmic). Commands: /caveman [lite|full|ultra|wenyan], /caveman-commit, /caveman-review, /caveman-stats, /caveman-compress <file>. Claimed: avg 65% output token reduction (range 22-87%, n=10 prompts), ~46% input reduction on compressed CLAUDE.md. README explicitly warns input/reasoning tokens untouched, whole-session savings smaller than output-only numbers, skill adds 1-1.5k input tokens/turn overhead, can go net-negative on already-terse workloads.

papers.title: Caveman: The Claude Code Skill That Cuts Token Use by 65%
papers.notes: Promotional coverage, no independent verification. Notes caveman is 'the wrong tool when output is consumed by humans' — recommended for agent-to-agent communication only.

papers.title: caveman: make Claude Code, Codex, and more agents say less fluff
papers.notes: Third-party usage guide for caveman skill install/config across multiple agent runtimes.

papers.title: Caveman | Lithic Token Compression (official landing page)
papers.notes: Official project site for the caveman skill (GitHub Pages).

papers.title: Caveman — the token-efficient stack for agent-native development
papers.notes: Separate/possibly mirrored marketing site for caveman-branded token-saving stack; distinguish from JuliusBrussee/caveman GitHub repo — needs cross-check in analysis stage for whether same project.

papers.title: DietrichGebert/ponytail — Makes your AI agent think like the laziest senior dev in the room
papers.notes: 7-rung 'lazy senior dev' decision ladder (necessity check -> codebase reuse -> standard library -> native platform -> existing dependency -> one-liner -> minimal working code). Rules: avoid unrequested abstractions, delete over add, shortest working diff wins, mark deliberate simplifications with 'ponytail:' comments, never sacrifice validation/error-handling/security/accessibility. SKILL.md itself contains no benchmark numbers — perf claims (up to 94% less code, ~54% mean across 12 tasks on FastAPI+React, Haiku 4.5 n=4) come from secondary blog coverage, not the repo itself. 74k-star claim per one secondary source (unverified against live repo).

papers.title: Ponytail: The AI Coding Skill That Saves Tokens by Writing Less Code
papers.notes: Secondary coverage of ponytail concept and philosophy.

papers.title: Ponytail: Make Your AI Agent Think Like the Laziest Senior Dev
papers.notes: Secondary coverage of ponytail.

papers.title: Ponytail: the AI Skill That Cuts 54% of the Code
papers.notes: Source of the 54% mean code-reduction figure referenced across secondary coverage.

papers.title: Ponytail: AI Agents Writing Less Code Like Senior Developers
papers.notes: Secondary coverage of ponytail.

papers.title: Ponytail Complete Guide: The 74k-Star AI Coding Philosophy
papers.notes: Secondary coverage; claims 74k GitHub stars for ponytail (unverified — cross-check against live repo star count in analysis stage).

papers.title: wilpel/caveman-compression — semantic compression method for LLM contexts
papers.notes: Distinct from JuliusBrussee/caveman despite shared 'caveman' branding — this is an algorithmic method (not a system prompt). Three modes: LLM-based (caveman_compress.py, 40-58% reduction via OpenAI API, ~2s/req), NLP-based (spaCy, rule-based, 15-30% reduction, offline, 15+ languages, <100ms), MLM-based (RoBERTa masked-LM, 20-30% reduction, offline, 1-5s). Benchmark: avg 40% reduction across test cases (58% on system prompts, 42% on API docs, 22% on resumes); factual preservation benchmark 13/13 facts (100%) preserved.

papers.title: Caveman Compression: Shrinking LLM Contexts Without Losing Meaning
papers.notes: Author's own writeup of wilpel/caveman-compression methodology and motivation.

papers.title: The Ultimate Token-Saving Stack: Headroom (RTK), Caveman, and TokenSave
papers.notes: Promotional combination piece: RTK/Headroom (stdout/stderr compressor, up to 90% claimed on CLI output), TokenSave (AST-based code query MCP, 60-80% claimed reduction in structural discovery), Caveman Mode (40-70% claimed chat prose reduction). No critique of real-world gap presented here — see codepointer.substack.com for the counter-analysis.

papers.title: Cutting LLM Token Costs with rtk, headroom, and caveman (critical replay analysis)
papers.notes: SOURCE OF THE 3.7% CRITIQUE. Replayed rtk+headroom+caveman counterfactually turn-by-turn over 500 sampled real Claude Code sessions (2,182 sessions across 13 project dirs, 614M tokens, $926.31 baseline spend) — combined real savings only 3.7% of spend vs 60-90% advertised. Three causes: (1) denominator mismatch — advertised % is per-payload, real bills are session-wide dominated by other costs; (2) workload mismatch — tools compress best on synthetic/repetitive data that's a small share of real traffic; (3) pricing structure — compressed tokens land in cheap cache_read ($0.50/M) while bills are dominated by cache_create (42%) and output (29%) which these tools don't touch.

papers.title: headroomlabs-ai/headroom — Compress tool outputs, logs, files, RAG chunks before they reach the LLM
papers.notes: 60-95% fewer tokens claimed, same answers. Library/proxy/MCP server modes; MCP tools: headroom_compress, headroom_retrieve, headroom_stats. Real-workload reductions cited: code search 92%, incident debugging 92%, issue triage 73%, codebase exploration 47%. Benchmarks: GSM8K accuracy delta ~0, TruthfulQA +0.030. Claimed 58.7k stars / 4.3k forks / 161 releases (self-reported via secondary source — verify directly).

papers.title: [FEATURE] Consider embedding TokenSave like done for RTK — headroom issue #1194
papers.notes: Ecosystem-integration discussion thread connecting headroom and tokensave projects — useful for tracing tool-composition history.

papers.title: aovestdipaperino/tokensave — code intelligence MCP server for AI coding agents (40+ tools, 30+ languages)
papers.notes: Rust port of CodeGraph (original TS impl by @colbymchenry). ~25MB binary, 50+ bundled tree-sitter grammars, 80+ symbol-level MCP tools (call graph, impact analysis, dead code detection, complexity ranking, etc.), 100% local, 9 agent integrations. Note: name/positioning overlaps with 'TokenSave' referenced in Hackenberger's stack article — verify same project in analysis stage.

papers.title: token-optimizer — AI Agents skill (SkillsLLM catalog entry)
papers.notes: 1,610 GitHub stars / 128 forks per catalog. Covers bloated configs, unused skills, stale memory, compaction loss, model misrouting, behavioral waste — claims cache-safe, measured savings. Works on Claude Code, OpenCode, OpenClaw, Codex, Hermes, GitHub Copilot (beta). Commands: /token-optimizer (audit), /token-coach (trend analysis). SQLite session DB + audit trail, real-time quality scoring. Passed SkillsLLM security scan.

papers.title: token-optimizer — Agent Skill (SkillsMP catalog mirror entry)
papers.notes: Possibly a distinct/forked token-optimizer skill from a different author than alexgreensh's — needs disambiguation in analysis stage (same name, different creator handle in catalog).

papers.title: Claw Compactor: LLM Token Compression Tool for AI Cost Saving (open-compress/claw-compactor)
papers.notes: Deterministic (no-LLM) 14-stage fusion pipeline (README describes evolution from an initial 5-layer design), AST-aware, reversible compression. Claims up to 97% token cost reduction. Built specifically for OpenClaw workspaces. pip install claw-compactor.

papers.title: SkillReducer: Optimizing LLM Agent Skills for Token Efficiency
papers.notes: Academic paper (not a repo/blog but directly relevant methodology): evaluated on 600 skills + SkillsBench benchmark. 48% mean compression on skill descriptions, 39% mean reduction on body tokens; compressed skills improved functional quality by 2.8% over originals — 'less is more' effect from removing non-essential content that distracts the context window. Directly informs the 'self-regulation via skill compression' angle of the research.

papers.title: CAVEWOMAN: How Large Language Models Behave Under Linguistic Input and Output Compression
papers.notes: Directly names and studies 'caveman style' compression academically (submitted 2026-06-23). Key finding: output compression saves cost (1.4-2.4x on API models) but INPUT compression backfires — 'strict lose-lose', raises net cost ~1.15x on 5-benchmark mean because models compensate for unclear/compressed input with longer responses while accuracy drops. ~half of generations preserve correctness despite surface divergence from unconstrained baseline. This is a strong independent academic counterpoint to the caveman-family tools' input-compression claims.

papers.title: Active Context Compression: Autonomous Memory Management in LLM Agents
papers.notes: 'Focus' system: capable models can autonomously self-regulate context when given compression tools + appropriate prompting — 6.0 autonomous compressions per task on average, up to 57% token savings on individual instances without sacrificing task performance. Relevant academic framing for 'self-regulation' mechanism design (vs external tool-imposed compression like caveman/ponytail/headroom).

papers.title: ContextBudget: Budget-Aware Context Management for Long-Horizon Search Agents
papers.notes: Formulates context-window compression as a budget-constrained sequential decision problem for long-horizon agents — compression decisions adapt dynamically to remaining context capacity. Relevant formal framing for token self-regulation as an optimization/policy problem rather than static rules (contrast with caveman/ponytail's static prompt-rule approach).
